title: सुब्रह्मण्य अष्टकम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री सुब्रह्मण्य अष्टकम् ॥

हे स्वामिनाथ करुणाकर दीनबन्धो
श्रीपार्वतीशमुखपङ्कजपद्मबन्धो।
श्रीशादिदेवगणपूजितपादपद्म
वल्लीसनाथ मम देहि करावलम्बम्॥१॥

देवादिदेवसुत देवगणाधिनाथ
देवेन्द्रवन्द्य मृदुपङ्कजमञ्जुपाद।
देवर्षिनारदमुनीन्द्रसुगीतकीर्ते
वल्लीसनाथ मम देहि करावलम्बम्॥२॥

नित्यान्नदान निरताखिल रोगहारिन्
तस्मात्प्रदान परिपूरितभक्तकाम।
श्रुत्यागमप्रणववाच्यनिजस्वरूप
वल्लीसनाथ मम देहि करावलम्बम्॥३॥

क्रौञ्चासुरेन्द्रपरिखण्डन शक्तिशूल
चापादिशस्त्रपरिमण्डितदिव्यपाणे।
श्रीकुण्डलीशधरतुण्डशिखीन्द्रवाह
वल्लीसनाथ मम देहि करावलम्बम्॥४॥

देवादिदेव रथमण्डल मध्यवेद्य
देवेन्द्रपीठनगरं दृढचापहस्तम्।
शूरं निहत्य सुरकोटिभिरीड्यमान
वल्लीसनाथ मम देहि करावलम्बम्॥५॥

हीरादिरत्नवर युक्तकिरीटहार
केयूरकुण्डलमणिश्रवणेन शोभम्।
हे वीर तारक जयाऽमरबृन्दवन्द्य
वल्लीसनाथ मम देहि करावलम्बम्॥६॥

पञ्चाक्षरादिमनुमन्त्रित गाङ्गतोयैः
पञ्चामृतैः प्रमुदिताचरणे नतानाम्।
पातालदारुण महोग्र भुजङ्ग भूत
वल्लीसनाथ मम देहि करावलम्बम्॥७॥

ध्यायाम्यहं प्रतिदिनं त्वदरूपमिष्टं
वल्लीशनाथ करुणाम्बुधिमादिदेवम्।
सन्तप्तरक्षणमशेषसुरारिनाशं
वल्लीसनाथ मम देहि करावलम्बम्॥८॥

॥ फलश्रुति ॥
सुब्रह्मण्याष्टकमिदं प्रातरुत्थाय यः पठेत्।
कोटिजन्मकृतं पापं तत्क्षणादेव नश्यति॥

॥ इति श्री सुब्रह्मण्य अष्टकं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shrī Subrahmaṇya Aṣṭakam ||

He Svāminātha karuṇākara dīnabandho
Śrīpārvatīśamukhapaṅkajapadmabandho.
Śrīśādidevaganapūjitapādapadma
Vallīsanātha mama dehi karāvalambam.||1||

Devādidevata devaganādhinātha
Devēndravandya mṛdupaṅkajamañjupāda.
DevarṣiNāradamunīndrasugītakīrte
Vallīsanātha mama dehi karāvalambam.||2||

Nityānnadāna niratākhila rogahārin
Tasmātpradāna paripūritabhaktakāma.
Śrutyāgamapraṇavavāchyanijasvarūpa
Vallīsanātha mama dehi karāvalambam.||3||

Krauñchāsurendraparikhaṇḍana śaktiśūla
Chāpādiśastraparimaṇḍitadivyapāṇe.
Śrīkuṇḍalīśadharatuṇḍaśikhīndravāha
Vallīsanātha mama dehi karāvalambam.||4||

Devādidevea rathamaṇḍala madhyavedya
Devēndrapīṭhanagaram dṛḍhachāpahastam.
Śūraṁ nihatya surakōṭibhirīḍyamāna
Vallīsanātha mama dehi karāvalambam.||5||

Hīrādiratnavara yuktakirīṭahāra
Keyūrakuṇḍalamaṇiśravaṇena śobham.
He Vīra Tāraka jayā'marabṛndavandya
Vallīsanātha mama dehi karāvalambam.||6||

Pañchākṣarādimanumantrrita gāṅgatoyaiḥ
Pañchāmṛtaiḥ pramuditācharaṇe natānām.
Pātāladāruṇa mahogra bhujaṅga bhūta
Vallīsanātha mama dehi karāvalambam.||7||

Dhyāyāmyahaṁ pratidinaṁ tvadarūpamiṣṭaṁ
Vallīśanātha karuṇāmbudhimādidēvam.
Santaptarakṣaṇamaśeṣasurārināśaṁ
Vallīsanātha mama dehi karāvalambam.||8||

|| Phalaśruti ||
Subrahmaṇyāṣṭakamidaṁ prātarutthāya yaḥ paṭhet.
Koṭijanmakṛtaṁ pāpaṁ tatkṣaṇādeva naśyati.

|| Iti Shrī Subrahmaṇya Aṣṭakaṁ sampūrṇam ||
hindiMeaning: हे स्वामीनाथ! करुणाकर! दीनबन्धु! श्री पार्वतीश (शिव) के मुख-कमल से उत्पन्न! श्री विष्णु आदि देवगणों द्वारा पूजित चरण-कमल वाले! वल्ली के नाथ! मुझे अपने हाथ का सहारा (आश्रय) दीजिए। देवों के देव के पुत्र! देवगणों के अधिनाथ! इन्द्र द्वारा वन्दनीय! कोमल कमल जैसे सुन्दर चरणों वाले! देवर्षि नारद और मुनीन्द्रों द्वारा गाई हुई कीर्ति वाले! वल्लीनाथ! मुझे अपने हाथ का सहारा दीजिए।
benefits: Traditionally recited for removal of sins accumulated over crores of lifetimes
Invokes the protective hand of lord subrahmanya
Classical tradition invokes this for removal of diseases and physical ailments
Traditionally recited for fulfillment of desires of devoted worshippers
Traditionally invoked for protection from serpent afflictions and evil spirits
Morning recitation brings instant spiritual purification
description: Subrahmanya Ashtakam — सुब्रह्मण्य अष्टकम् — is an eight-verse devotional hymn offered to Lord Kartikeya. This revered text originates from Traditional Sanskrit Hymn. Devotees recite this prayer to remove all diseases and physical ailments and protect from serpent afflictions and evil spirits. It is also said to grant the protective hand of Lord Subrahmanya. This sacred text is ideally recited on Tuesday (Mangalvar) and during Kartik Purnima for enhanced blessings. The text contains 8 verses and takes around 8 minutes to recite, fitting conveniently into a daily spiritual routine. On this page, you can read the complete Subrahmanya Ashtakam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Subrahmanya Ashtakam in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Eight sacred verses to Lord Kartikeya for divine protection and spiritual purification.
relatedSearches: Subrahmanya Ashtakam for sarpa dosha remedy
Best Subrahmanya stotras for serpent affliction

seoFaqs.question: What is the Subrahmanya Ashtakam and what does it mean?
seoFaqs.answer: The Subrahmanya Ashtakam (सुब्रह्मण्य अष्टकम्) is an eight-verse devotional hymn (ashtakam) addressed to Lord Subrahmanya — also known as Kartikeya, Murugan, Skanda, Kumara and Shanmukha — the warrior-son of Lord Shiva and Devi Parvati, leader of the celestial army, slayer of the asura Tarakasura and Krauncha, and consort of Devi Valli and Devasena. The hymn is composed in the karavalambam (hand-grasping) genre — each of its eight verses ends with the moving refrain 'Vallisanatha mama dehi karavalambam' — 'O Lord of Valli, give me the support of your hand.' The composition is preserved in the classical Sanskrit hymn tradition and is widely chanted by devotees of Murugan in Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Karnataka and Andhra Pradesh as a daily Kartikeya prayer for protection, removal of disease, removal of serpent afflictions and the grace of Subrahmanya. (Sources: classical Subrahmanya stotra tradition; Skanda upasana paddhati.)

seoFaqs.question: What does Vallisanatha mama dehi karavalambam mean?
seoFaqs.answer: Vallisanatha mama dehi karavalambam — the famous closing refrain of every verse of the Subrahmanya Ashtakam — is one of the most beloved prayers of Murugan bhakti. Word-by-word: Valli — the tribal-girl bride of Murugan, daughter of the hunter-chief, who won the Lord's heart through pure unconditioned love and is one of his two consorts (along with Devasena); sa-natha — having (sa) as her Lord (natha); Vallisa-natha — therefore 'O Lord of Valli'; mama — to me; dehi — give, grant; kara-avalambam — the support (avalambam) of your hand (kara). The verse is the cry of a helpless devotee falling at the Lord's feet — 'O Lord of Valli, please give me the support of your hand to lift me up; I am sinking and only your grasp can save me.' This karavalambam genre, perfected by Sri Adi Shankaracharya, is a hallmark of supreme prapatti (surrender) bhakti. (Sources: classical karavalambam stotra tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is Subrahmanya invoked for removal of serpent (sarpa) afflictions?
seoFaqs.answer: Lord Subrahmanya is identified across the South Indian agama tradition with the Naga (serpent) principle and with the kundalini-shakti coiled at the muladhara chakra. The connection has multiple sources: (1) at Subrahmanya Kshetra (Kukke Subrahmanya in Karnataka) Lord Subrahmanya is worshipped explicitly as the protector and lord of all serpents, with the principal deity treated as Naga-raja Vasuki who took refuge with Skanda after defeat by Garuda; (2) Subrahmanya is seen as Skanda-Naga, the great protector against sarpa-dosha (serpent-affliction in the natal chart, indicating Rahu-Ketu and Kuja-Naga doshas), against snake-bite injury, and against fear of serpents; (3) verse 7 of the Subrahmanya Ashtakam — 'Patala-daruna mahogra-bhujanga-bhuta' — explicitly invokes Subrahmanya as the destroyer of patala-dwelling serpent-spirits. Devotees with kala-sarpa-dosha, naga-dosha or who have killed serpents are traditionally directed to Subrahmanya parayana, Subrahmanya kshetra-darshana and naga-pratishtha. (Sources: Skanda Purana; Kukke Subrahmanya kshetra mahatmya.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the significance of the opening verse He Svaminatha Karunakara Dinabandho?
seoFaqs.answer: The opening verse — He Svaminatha karunakara dina-bandho, Sripaarvati-isha-mukha-pankaja-padmabandho, Sri-isha-adi-deva-gana-pujita-pada-padma, Vallisanatha mama dehi kara-avalambam — sets the entire devotional tone of the ashtakam. The devotee addresses Subrahmanya by his most heart-melting epithets: He Svaminatha — O Master, O Lord; karuna-akara — the very mine and ocean of compassion; dina-bandho — the brother and friend of the wretched and helpless; Sri-Parvati-isha (Shiva)-mukha-pankaja-padma-bandho — the friend (companion-flower) of the lotus-faces of Parvati and Shiva (i.e. born from their union and beloved by both); Sri-isha (Vishnu)-adi-deva-gana-pujita-pada-padma — whose lotus-feet are worshipped by Vishnu, Brahma and all the gods; followed by the universal refrain Vallisanatha mama dehi karavalambam. The verse establishes Subrahmanya as the supreme refuge of the helpless — the karunakara who never abandons one who calls. (Sources: Subrahmanya Ashtakam verse 1; classical commentary.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the phalashruti and benefit of reciting the Subrahmanya Ashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: The phalashruti of the Subrahmanya Ashtakam is among the most powerful in the karavalambam genre: 'Subrahmanya-ashtakam-idam pratar-utthaya yah pathet, koti-janma-kritam papam tatkshanad-eva nashyati' — 'Whoever rises at dawn and recites this Subrahmanya Ashtakam — the sins committed across crores of births are destroyed in that very instant.' Beyond this supreme phala, traditional teachers attribute these specific benefits: removal of all diseases and physical ailments through verse 3 (Akhila-rogahaarin); protection from serpent-afflictions and evil spirits through verse 7 (Patala-daruna mahogra-bhujanga-bhuta); victory over enemies and inner asuras like Krauncha and Tarakasura through verses 4-5; the grasp of the Lord's hand for one who has fallen and cannot rise on his own; and the bhakta-kama-paripurana — fulfilment of the devoted worshipper's righteous desires — through verse 3. Daily morning recitation is the classical sadhana. (Sources: Subrahmanya Ashtakam phalashruti; classical Skanda upasana.)

seoFaqs.question: When is the best time to recite the Subrahmanya Ashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: The Subrahmanya Ashtakam is most powerful when recited on the following occasions: (1) Tuesday (Mangalvar) — the day of Mangal/Kuja and traditionally associated with the warrior-deity Subrahmanya in many South Indian traditions, especially for sarpa-dosha and Mangal-dosha remedy; (2) Skanda Shashthi — the supreme Subrahmanya festival of Kartik Shukla Shashthi, commemorating Skanda's victory over Tarakasura, with parayana on each of the six preceding days; (3) Thaipusam — Pushya nakshatra of Tamil month Thai (January-February), the great Murugan festival celebrated with kavadi-attam at Palani, Tiruchendur and Batu Caves; (4) Vaikasi Visakam — Visakha nakshatra of Tamil month Vaikasi (May-June), the appearance day of Murugan; (5) every Krittika nakshatra (since Subrahmanya is also Karttikeya, son of the six Krittika mothers); (6) Kartik Purnima; and (7) any visit to a Subrahmanya kshetra — Tiruchendur, Tiruparankundram, Palani, Tiruttani, Swamimalai, Pazhamudircholai or Kukke Subrahmanya. (Sources: classical Skanda parayana paddhati.)
